header.title: Linked List
header.description: A linked list stores elements as independent nodes scattered in memory, each holding a value and a pointer to the next node. Unlike arrays there is no contiguous block — indexing costs O(n), but inserting or deleting at a known node costs O(1) with no shifting required.

howItWorks.title: How linked lists work
items: Each node stores two things: a value and a next pointer to the following node. The last node's next is null, marking the end of the list.
The list is anchored by a head pointer. Traversal always starts at head and follows next pointers one by one — there is no shortcut to index n.
Prepending (insert at head) is O(1): create a node, point its next to the current head, then update head.
Appending to the tail is O(1) with a tail pointer, or O(n) without one — you must walk the entire list to find the last node.
Inserting or deleting in the middle requires traversing to the right position first (O(n)), then updating two pointers — no elements are shifted.
Because nodes are not contiguous, linked lists are cache-unfriendly compared to arrays — each pointer hop may cause a cache miss.

linkedListTypes.label: Singly Linked List
linkedListTypes.description: Each node holds a value and one next pointer. The simplest form — minimal memory overhead and straightforward traversal, but movement is one-directional only.
characteristics: One pointer per node — lowest memory footprint
Forward traversal only — cannot go backward
Delete requires the predecessor node to re-link
Prepend O(1), all else O(n) without extra bookkeeping
useCases: Undo history where only forward replay matters

linkedListTypes.label: Doubly Linked List
linkedListTypes.description: Each node has both next and prev pointers. Backward traversal is free, and deleting a known node is O(1) because you already have the predecessor via prev.
characteristics: Two pointers per node — slightly more memory
Bidirectional traversal — iterate forward or backward
O(1) delete at any known node — no predecessor search needed
Used by Java LinkedList, Python collections.deque, browser history

linkedListTypes.label: Circular Singly Linked List
linkedListTypes.description: Tail's next pointer wraps back to head instead of null. Traversal never truly ends — you cycle through nodes indefinitely until you detect you've returned to the start.
characteristics: Any node can act as the start point
Detect full cycle by checking if curr.next === head
Efficient for repeatedly cycling through a fixed set

linkedListTypes.label: Circular Doubly Linked List
linkedListTypes.description: Combines doubly linked and circular properties — every node has prev and next, and the tail and head are connected in both directions. Maximum flexibility at maximum pointer cost.
characteristics: Traverse forward or backward from any node
O(1) insert and delete anywhere with a node reference
Used by the Linux kernel CFS scheduler and many OS task queues

footerAlerts.title: When linked lists shine
items: Frequent insert/delete at the head or a known node — O(1) with no shifting.
Implementing stacks and queues — push/pop map directly to prepend/deleteHead.
Unknown or highly variable size — no pre-allocation or resizing needed.
Classic interview patterns: reverse, detect cycle (Floyd's), merge two sorted lists, find the middle node.

footerAlerts.title: When to consider alternatives
items: Random access by index → use an array (O(1) vs O(n)).
Cache-sensitive iteration over large datasets → arrays have far better locality.
Frequent search by value → use a hash set/map for O(1) average lookup.

hints: Always clarify whether a tail pointer is maintained — it changes append from O(n) to O(1).
Draw the pointer updates before coding any insertion or deletion to avoid off-by-one bugs.
Edge cases to always handle: empty list, single-node list, operating on head or tail.
